title: Staff Infection

jokes.text: Michael's Saturday morning 'bike ride' with his son — Michael is driving a slow car alongside George Michael on a bike, multitasking work calls instead of being present
jokes.setup: Narrator describes Michael's cherished Saturday bike ride with his son as a bonding tradition
jokes.punchline: The 'bike ride' consists of Michael driving alongside him slowly in a car while George Michael insists he can ride alone — and Michael immediately answers a work call
jokes.explanation: The layered irony is excellent — Michael believes he's being a good father while completely undermining the activity. The visual of a grown man driving at bicycle-pace beside his son encapsulates his character's tragic blind spot perfectly. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Michael answers his phone mid-sentence to his son, causing Buster (on a bike nearby) to crash
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to be a present father on the bike ride but keeps getting distracted
jokes.punchline: He answers his phone, abandons attention to the road, and an unseen Buster crashes — Michael casually says 'Nice instincts, son. Good reflexes.'
jokes.explanation: The sudden slapstick of Buster crashing (inferred) combined with Michael's oblivious praise of his son for reflexes he didn't use is a tight, well-structured gag. Michael praising George Michael for Buster's crash is a great misattribution joke. 'Good reflexes' lands perfectly.

jokes.text: Michael takes a work call mid-bike-ride, nearly causes a crash, then says 'Nice instincts, son. Good reflexes.'
jokes.setup: Michael abandons the bike ride for a phone call, causing an accident hazard
jokes.punchline: Michael praises George Michael's reflexes as if narrowly avoiding a car accident caused by his own negligence is a parenting win
jokes.explanation: Michael's oblivious parenting is perfectly encapsulated here — he creates the danger and then takes credit for George Michael surviving it. The deadpan delivery of 'Good reflexes' as a genuine compliment is the whole joke.

jokes.text: Tobias practicing his frightened inmate role: 'I ain't going to squeal, man. I ain't going to... Oh, I'm not frightened! God!'
jokes.setup: Tobias has been cast as a 'frightened inmate' and is rehearsing his lines at home
jokes.punchline: He delivers the bravado line and then immediately breaks character to complain that he's not frightened — he's doing the opposite of what the role requires
jokes.explanation: Perfectly character-specific — Tobias's narcissism and obliviousness prevent him from accessing any genuine emotion. The snap from tough-guy bravado to offended actor is beautifully absurd and wholly Tobias. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Lindsay, say something to scare me. ...me.
jokes.setup: Tobias asks Lindsay to say something scary so he can practice his frightened reaction
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's single-word answer — 'me' — is her drily suggesting that she herself is what scares him, delivered flatly and ignored
jokes.explanation: Extraordinary economy — one word does everything. It reveals the dysfunctional marriage, Lindsay's self-awareness, and Tobias's total dismissal of her all at once. The pause before 'Nope. Nothing. Thanks for trying though.' makes the punchline land harder. Among the episode's best constructions.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Nope. Nothing. Thanks for trying though.' after Lindsay's attempt to frighten him
jokes.setup: Tobias asked Lindsay to scare him; she offered herself as the scary thing
jokes.punchline: Tobias's total non-reaction — genuine, oblivious appreciation for the effort — makes the dismissal of Lindsay even more devastating
jokes.explanation: Works as the second beat of joke 4's setup. The polite sincerity of 'Thanks for trying though' is perfectly Tobias — he genuinely appreciates the attempt while being completely impervious. Scores slightly lower than joke 4 as it's the payoff rather than the construction.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'I ought to check myself into a men's penal colony. Perhaps your father could get me in.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is frustrated with his inability to feel fear and is musing about method acting
jokes.punchline: 'Penal colony' is a Tobias double entendre (he means prison for method acting research, the audience hears something else), and 'perhaps your father could get me in' adds the layer that George Sr. is actually in prison
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in layered Arrested Development comedy. 'Penal colony' is a classic Tobias accidental double entendre — the character means it sincerely, the audience reads it sexually. But the joke has a THIRD layer: George Sr. is literally in prison and could legitimately 'get him in.' Three simultaneous meanings, perfect character voice, completely earned. Justifies the high score.

jokes.text: Annyong's name is revealed to not be a name but the Korean word for 'hello' — every time he 'says his name' he's just greeting people
jokes.setup: Lucille introduces her adopted Korean son 'Annyong' as if that's his name
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals 'Annyong' is simply the Korean word for 'hello' — the family has been calling him 'Hello' this entire time
jokes.explanation: This is a running gag with a brilliant linguistic foundation. The reveal recontextualizes every prior interaction with the character — Lucille thought she was addressing him by name, he was just saying hello. The layered cultural obliviousness of the Bluth family (especially Lucille) is perfectly captured. Rewatch value is enormous as every previous 'Annyong' exchange becomes funnier.

jokes.text: Lucille on Annyong's work ethic: 'unlike some sons I know' / Buster: 'I'm a scholar. I enjoy scholarly pursuits.'
jokes.setup: Lucille praises Annyong's work ethic at the banana stand
jokes.punchline: Her immediate passive-aggressive dig at Buster, and Buster's defense — calling himself a 'scholar' — is absurd given that he is a grown, unemployed adult living with his mother
jokes.explanation: Standard Lucille-Buster dynamic, well-executed. Buster calling himself a 'scholar' to deflect from doing literally nothing is good character comedy. Not exceptional but functionally solid.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Suddenly playing with yourself is a scholarly pursuit.'
jokes.setup: Buster has just declared himself a scholar who enjoys scholarly pursuits
jokes.punchline: Lucille's withering comeback implies Buster literally masturbates as his 'scholarly' activity — delivered with patrician cruelty
jokes.explanation: Lucille at her most vicious. The phrasing is perfect — 'suddenly' implies she's been watching this evolve, 'scholarly pursuit' mirrors Buster's own pompous word choice. The casual maternal cruelty is wholly in character. Strong quotability — a line you'd repeat.

jokes.text: Buster panics at the mention of Catalina because of 'an unfortunate encounter in a photo booth on the wildlife-populated island of Catalina'
jokes.setup: Someone mentions Catalina and Buster visibly panics; the narrator provides vague context
jokes.punchline: The explanation — 'unfortunate encounter in a photo booth on the wildlife-populated island of Catalina' — raises more questions than it answers, and the specificity of 'wildlife-populated' is perfectly ominous
jokes.explanation: AD's technique of providing 'explanations' that explain nothing is on full display. The specificity of 'photo booth' and 'wildlife-populated' creates vivid but deliberately incomplete horror. The joke is the narrator's carefully chosen vagueness. Rewatch value as a potential foreshadow.

jokes.text: Lucille about Lupe's family reunion bus: 'We can't have them hanging around like freeloaders, looking for an easy buck.' — said while she herself is about to ask Michael for a paycheck she doesn't earn
jokes.setup: Lucille is outraged that Lupe's family might use the Bluth Company parking lot
jokes.punchline: The immediate hypocrisy — Lucille is literally about to ask Michael for a free paycheck — makes this dramatic irony rather than intentional wit
jokes.explanation: The joke relies on irony that pays off 30 seconds later when Lucille asks for money. Works better in context than in isolation. Good character consistency but not especially clever construction.

jokes.text: Michael: 'You get a paycheck from the Bluth Company?' / Lucille: 'It's important to the company that I keep up the image of my lifestyle.' / Michael: 'Illusion, Mom.'
jokes.setup: Lucille asks Michael to retrieve her paycheck from the company
jokes.punchline: Michael corrects her use of 'image' with 'illusion' — not only is it more accurate about the Bluths' façade, it's a perfect character beat showing Michael's own complicity in maintaining the fiction
jokes.explanation: The word substitution from 'image' to 'illusion' is quietly devastating. Michael's correction is both semantically precise (Lucille has no actual income, only the appearance of wealth) and reveals his own knowledge of and participation in the family's deceptions. A small but very sharp moment.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'I believe I'm the one that asked you to start shopping at Quantity Plus. They made me apply for a membership card. It was humiliating.'
jokes.setup: Michael is confronting his mother about unearned paychecks
jokes.punchline: Lucille pivots to complaining that being asked to apply for a discount warehouse club membership card was 'humiliating' — the magnitude of her entitlement is the joke
jokes.explanation: Solid Lucille character moment. The pivot from serious financial accountability to petty class grievance is a reliable AD move. 'Humiliating' is perfectly calibrated to her character. Not exceptional but very consistent.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Does that mean I have to go to your office to get mine, too, or how's that work?'
jokes.setup: Michael has told Lucille her paycheck is the last he'll send to the house; Buster has been silent in the background
jokes.punchline: Buster reveals he ALSO receives unearned paychecks from the Bluth Company — his casual inquiry about logistics is completely oblivious to how outrageous this is
jokes.explanation: A great surprise escalation — just when Michael thinks he's made his point to Lucille, Buster pipes up with the same problem. The logistics question ('or how's that work?') is perfectly oblivious. Buster's innocence about the absurdity of his situation is the engine.

jokes.text: Michael tells Buster he'll have to work in the copy room to earn his money. Buster: 'Okay. I accept. I'll take that job. I will work in the copy room.'
jokes.setup: Michael is threatening Buster with actual employment as a consequence
jokes.punchline: Buster accepts with formal, earnest gravity — 'I will work in the copy room' — treating a menial office task as if it's a solemn professional commitment
jokes.explanation: Buster's solemn acceptance is a good character beat. The repetition — 'I'll take that job. I will work in the copy room.' — reinforces his earnestness. Functional comedy.

jokes.text: 'I wouldn't go in there without knocking, Michael.' — said about the copy room where Buster has been assigned
jokes.setup: The copy room is presented as a normal office space
jokes.punchline: Someone warns Michael not to enter without knocking — implying something inappropriate has already happened involving Buster and/or whoever was there before him
jokes.explanation: A well-placed throwaway that implies something happening off-screen. The economy is excellent — no explanation needed. Gets better on rewatch when you know Buster's claustrophobia will be a plot point.

jokes.text: Michael's staff meeting: 'Now, I know we've been putting in a lot of hours, but we've got to keep our heads down and power through, you know, and... sacrifice.' — and Lindsay walks in demanding a paycheck in the middle of it
jokes.setup: Michael is giving a sincere, slightly tiresome 'power through' speech to his employees
jokes.punchline: Lindsay walks in, completely unmoved by or even aware of the speech, casually asking for her paycheck
jokes.explanation: The interruption of the speech by its living antithesis (Lindsay) is a reliable sitcom structure. Works because the show has established Michael's 'power through' mantra as a verbal tic earlier in this same episode, making Lindsay's intrusion funnier.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'Dad hired me out of college.' Michael: 'You quit college.' Lindsay: 'Yeah, well, I had a job. What was the point?'
jokes.setup: Michael asks how long Lindsay has been on the payroll
jokes.punchline: Lindsay says she was hired 'out of college' — then immediately reveals she quit college for the job, making 'out of college' technically but absurdly true
jokes.explanation: A three-beat exchange with perfect structure. 'Hired out of college' sets up a normal expectation; 'you quit college' deflates it; 'I had a job, what was the point?' delivers the final twist — she quit college for a job she's never actually done. The logical consistency of Lindsay's illogic is the engine. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Lindsay leaves the staff meeting when Michael says 'staff' — 'Well, you said staff.'
jokes.setup: Michael tells Lindsay to go back to her desk and 'be like Buster'; then calls the meeting back to order for 'the staff'
jokes.punchline: Lindsay interprets 'staff' as a different word — likely the British slang — and uses it as an excuse to leave
jokes.explanation: A very economical wordplay joke — 'Well, you said staff' is Lindsay using a possible double meaning as a logical out. The AD writers leave it just ambiguous enough to be funny. Lindsay's instinct to find ANY excuse to leave work is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Lindsay to Michael: 'You and I have different management styles. I believe work should be fun, and you try to crush people's spirits. What's next, Michael? Are you going to make dancing illegal? Is this the tiny town from Footloose?'
jokes.setup: Michael is frustrated that Lindsay won't be serious about the business
jokes.punchline: Lindsay compares a normal workplace expectation to the villainous authority in Footloose — the analogy is absurd and self-righteous, but an employee says 'Good point'
jokes.explanation: The Footloose reference is fairly dated pop-culture comedy, but it's redeemed by the employee's sincere 'Good point' — which implies Michael's workers genuinely sympathize with Lindsay's rebellion. Character integration is solid. A little bloated.

jokes.text: Buster's escalating panic in the copy room — calling out 'Bob? Bob? Bob?' alone in the small space
jokes.setup: Buster has confidently accepted the copy room job; narrator reveals he 'grew anxious in the confined space'
jokes.punchline: Buster is alone in the copy room calling out for the person who quit — the fear is entirely psychological and self-generated
jokes.explanation: The image of Buster alone, calling out for a man who no longer works there, in a tiny room he's voluntarily entered, is quietly absurd and perfectly Buster. The payoff — he needs 'a different job' — sets up the outdoor/indoor escalation.

jokes.text: Buster: 'I need a different job. I'm having real trouble in a confined indoor space.' Worker: 'How do you feel about working outdoors?' Buster: 'Uh, what else do you have?'
jokes.setup: Buster has confessed to claustrophobia and been offered an outdoor alternative
jokes.punchline: Buster rejects outdoors too — revealing his phobia is not just confined spaces but open spaces as well (confirmed moments later by Michael: 'It's both')
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation. The joke pivots from a straightforward phobia joke (claustrophobia) to the revelation that the solution (outdoors) is also feared. 'What else do you have?' implies Buster has been living in some impossible middle space between inside and outside. The casual delivery makes it land harder. Strong Buster character work.

jokes.text: Maeby to George Michael about Michael: 'I guess he just likes work more than he likes you.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is defending his father's absence to Maeby
jokes.punchline: Maeby cuts through his rationalization with blunt cruelty that is also probably true
jokes.explanation: Maeby is the show's designated truth-teller and this line exemplifies it. The economy is superb — one sentence does the work of a whole arc. Slightly undercut by being a familiar sitcom sentiment, but the delivery in context (George Michael has been straining to defend his dad) makes it land.

jokes.text: Tobias arrives at prison with a letter of introduction from Carl Weathers
jokes.setup: Tobias is trying to research his inmate role by going to an actual prison
jokes.punchline: His credential is a letter from Carl Weathers — an actor, not a prison official — which somehow works on the warden
jokes.explanation: The Carl Weathers callback is a series-level running gag. The absurdity of a letter from an actor granting prison access, and it WORKING, is delightfully nonsensical. The warden's reverence for 'the arts' is a good lampshade on the absurdity.

jokes.text: The warden: 'It would be an honor to have you research in this institution.' then immediately hands Tobias a script to pass to Carl Weathers: 'The warden could easily be black.'
jokes.setup: The warden is graciously hosting Tobias as a visiting actor/researcher
jokes.punchline: The warden has his own script he wants to get to Carl Weathers — and his self-pitching note 'the warden could easily be black' reveals he wants to play himself in a movie
jokes.explanation: 'The warden could easily be black' is a quotable, original joke that works on multiple levels — it's a Hollywood note about race-blind casting, it reveals the warden's vanity and Hollywood dreams, and it's structurally perfect as the payoff to his gracious hosting. The specificity of the self-insert is what elevates it from a generic Hollywood joke.

jokes.text: The warden lists available cells: '187 won't be free till Tuesday... at midnight. And I can't put you into 212, because there's already another actor researching a role in there... Steve Buscemi.'
jokes.setup: The warden is trying to find Tobias a cell in the prison
jokes.punchline: The reveal that Steve Buscemi — a real, named celebrity — is already in a cell method-acting is a perfect absurdist joke. Tobias's crestfallen 'Re...' before composing himself is a great beat.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The logic is airtight — in a world where Tobias can get into prison for research via a Carl Weathers letter, of course Steve Buscemi would already be there doing the same. Tobias's immediate jealousy ('Re...') before pivoting is perfectly played. The specificity of 'Steve Buscemi' versus a fictional actor makes it sing.

jokes.text: Tobias requests to bunk with his father-in-law (George Sr., who is an actual prisoner). The warden says 'Okay.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is being assigned a cell as a method-acting researcher; George Sr. is serving real prison time
jokes.punchline: Tobias casually requests to bunk with his father-in-law (a real felon) and the warden accommodates without comment
jokes.explanation: The warden's casual 'Okay' to what should be a highly irregular request is good deadpan. The joke relies on the established absurdism of the prison scenes. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Tobias, given prison clothes, gasps with pure excitement: 'Wow! Prison!'
jokes.setup: Tobias has just been dressed in prison garb as a method actor
jokes.punchline: His reaction is child-like wonder — 'Wow! Prison!' — with no trace of irony or fear
jokes.explanation: Two words, maximum character reveal. Tobias's unguarded enthusiasm for prison is the logical endpoint of his method-acting self-delusion. The economy is near-perfect. It's essentially a character thesis statement in the form of an exclamation.

jokes.text: Gob arrives at the construction site claiming Kitty 'is starting to sound pretty damn sexy to me' over the phone — then Michael reveals it was Lindsay
jokes.setup: Gob says the voice on the phone who told him where Michael was sounded sexy to him
jokes.punchline: That 'sexy' voice was his sister Lindsay — Gob's attraction to his own sister is presented as a normal punchline beat
jokes.explanation: The misdirection works and the Bluth family's dysfunctionality is efficiently highlighted. The 'anyway' pivot from both characters afterward is a good deadpan recovery. Somewhat familiar territory for the show.

jokes.text: Michael to the foreman about Buster: 'I got Buster here to help.' Foreman: 'That's another guy.'
jokes.setup: Michael is explaining how he's covering for the payroll delay by providing Buster as labor
jokes.punchline: The foreman's flat correction — 'that's another guy' — implies Buster is so obviously not a construction worker that having him on site actually makes things worse, not better
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly economical line. 'That's another guy' means: Buster is not a labor asset, he's a liability requiring management. The understatement does everything; nothing is over-explained. This is how AD does its best deadpan — maximum information in minimum words.

jokes.text: Buster reports the workers' profanity to Michael with wonder: 'One of the guys told me to take my head out of my bottom and get back to work. My bottom!'
jokes.setup: Buster has been mingling with the construction workers and apparently been cursed at
jokes.punchline: The profanity was so sanitized for Buster's ears (or in Buster's recounting) that 'bottom' replaces the obvious word — and Buster is delighted rather than offended
jokes.explanation: This is peak Buster. The euphemism 'bottom' for 'ass' and his delighted, awed repetition — 'My bottom!' — is a character-perfect joke. The joke captures his infantilization perfectly: he has the vocabulary of a toddler and the delight of someone experiencing the adult world for the first time. High quotability.

jokes.text: Gob yells 'Pick up the pace, douche bag!' at workers; Michael echoes 'You too, candy ass!' then pauses and says 'Candy ass.'
jokes.setup: Gob is playing tough foreman at the construction site; Michael begrudgingly joins in
jokes.punchline: Michael shouts 'candy ass' — clearly uncomfortable with the word — and then quietly repeats it to himself as if tasting it for the first time
jokes.explanation: The quiet repetition of 'candy ass' is Michael's moment — he's trying on a version of himself he doesn't recognize. The self-conscious savoring of the insult is a perfect character beat. Economy in that single repeated word does all the work.

jokes.text: Lindsay announces she's taking the whole staff out to lunch on a Saturday, to office-wide cheers
jokes.setup: Lindsay has been left nominally in charge; the employees tentatively ask for two pizza toppings on account of it being Saturday
jokes.punchline: Lindsay immediately escalates — taking the whole staff to a restaurant — to be the cool boss, in contrast to Michael's 'power through' ethos
jokes.explanation: Functional setup for the payoff when the employees end up in Catalina. The comedy here is mostly structural — the joke lands later when Lindsay has lost the sheep. On its own, it's a competent setup.

jokes.text: Lindsay turns off the office lights: 'Doesn't this just wanna make you curl up and forget about the world?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is 'managing' the office in Michael's absence
jokes.punchline: She turns off the fluorescent lights and suggests that forgetting about the world is a desirable workplace outcome — her management philosophy in one line
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's therapy-speak applied to workplace management is very in character. 'Curl up and forget about the world' as a management goal is a nicely absurdist articulation of her worldview. Solid.

jokes.text: Michael: 'They're sheep. They'll wander off, and you'll lose the sheep.' Lindsay: 'They'll be fine.' — cut to employees boarding Lupe's family reunion bus thinking Lindsay chartered it for lunch
jokes.setup: Michael warns Lindsay the employees need leadership or they'll wander off
jokes.punchline: They literally wander onto the wrong bus (headed to Catalina for a Mexican family reunion) because Lindsay had no plan
jokes.explanation: A masterfully constructed multi-scene payoff. The episode set up Lupe's family reunion bus in the parking lot earlier; now Michael's literal warning ('you'll lose the sheep') immediately comes true in the most specific way possible. The structural precision — the exact same parking lot, the exact same bus — makes this an AD classic callback. The show earns this joke through proper setup.

jokes.text: Michael: 'All right? They'll wander off, and you'll lose the sheep. You've lost the sheep.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has told employees to 'figure something out' for transportation to lunch with no plan
jokes.punchline: Michael predicts they'll 'lose the sheep,' and then the punch: 'You've lost the sheep' — confirming it immediately
jokes.explanation: Nice quick setup-punchline where Michael's prediction is immediately confirmed. The sheep metaphor is funny in Michael's mouth — he's both right and condescending simultaneously.

jokes.text: Lindsay shows Michael the completed proposal: 'It's right here, Michael. You weren't here and everything got done while I was in charge. See? Not so hard, what you do.'
jokes.setup: Michael has returned fearing chaos; Lindsay presents completed work as proof of her competence
jokes.punchline: Everything DID get done — but in the process she sent all the employees to Catalina on a wrong bus, which hasn't been discovered yet
jokes.explanation: Dramatic irony works here — the audience knows what Lindsay doesn't yet. Her confident 'not so hard, what you do' lands because we know the hidden cost of her 'success.' Functional comedy rather than exceptional craft.

jokes.text: Michael: 'You really think I could leave here and everything would be fine?' Lindsay: 'I think everything would be fine but you.'
jokes.setup: Michael is fishing for validation that he's indispensable
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's response flips it — the company would be fine, Michael would not be — correctly diagnosing his workaholic dependency
jokes.explanation: Elegant structural inversion. Lindsay reframes Michael's question in a way that's both a comedy punchline and actual psychological insight. The economy is superb — one sentence does what a therapy scene would take minutes to establish. Among the more quotable lines in the episode.

jokes.text: Michael: 'If I didn't have that phone call from the zoning committee—' Lindsay: 'You'd have no reason to live. I'll have it forwarded to your cell.'
jokes.setup: Michael is making excuses about why he can't leave the office
jokes.punchline: Lindsay escalates his excuse to an existential diagnosis and immediately offers the practical solution that removes his last rationalization
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's 'you'd have no reason to live' lands as dark comedy because it's probably accurate, and then the immediate practical offer to forward the call strips away Michael's last defense. The structure is perfect — diagnosis, then solution, then Michael has nowhere to hide. One of Lindsay's sharpest moments in the episode.

jokes.text: Cut to the surveillance team after the employees leave: 'The place is empty. All right. Let's get in there and bring this company down.' — directly echoing Lindsay's 'Like that's even possible'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has just said 'like that's even possible' about bringing the company down, as Michael worries
jokes.punchline: An FBI surveillance team appears, literally saying 'let's bring this company down' — making Lindsay's dismissal immediately ironic
jokes.explanation: This is a textbook AD construction: a character makes a dismissive joke, the scene cuts immediately to the literal version of that joke being executed. The structural payoff is precise and the stakes (federal investigation) add genuine dramatic irony. The rewatch value is high because on second viewing you catch the setup more clearly. The structure score is among the episode's highest.

jokes.text: George Sr. converting inmates to Judaism in prison while a gang leader threatens him for 'trying to convert my team'
jokes.setup: George Sr. has been in prison and the narrator says he's 'making converts to his new way of life'
jokes.punchline: He's running Jewish conversion sessions in a maximum security prison and has annoyed a gang leader whose 'team' is his gang
jokes.explanation: The premise is absurd and character-consistent — George Sr. has found a hustle even in prison. The threat framed as franchise protection ('my team') adds dark comedy. 'LittleJustice' as a gang name is a nice detail.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'Both of our religions have a lot to offer. There's the Jewish notion of heaven and that it can be attained here on Earth... and there is your belief in the cleansing power of the pipe.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is trying to defuse tension with a gang leader by suggesting religious common ground
jokes.punchline: His 'ecumenical' outreach equates Jewish theology with gang violence (a 'pipe' beating) as equivalent 'beliefs' — the false equivalence is deliberately absurd
jokes.explanation: George Sr.'s con-man logic applied to interfaith dialogue is a perfect character joke. The parallel structure — 'Jewish notion of heaven' vs. 'cleansing power of the pipe' — is formally precise and the gap between the two is the entire joke. George Sr.'s ability to frame anything as a negotiation is essential to his character. Strong originality.

jokes.text: Gang leader: 'No more teaching from you.' George Sr.: 'No teaching. No teaching.' — frantically agreeing with his own prohibition
jokes.setup: The gang leader has threatened George Sr. and banned him from converting more members
jokes.punchline: George Sr. desperately agrees — 'No teaching. No teaching.' — the repetition suggesting he's trying to convince himself as much as the gang leader
jokes.explanation: A solid quick beat. George Sr.'s frightened compliance is funny after his earlier pomposity, but it's more of a grace note after joke 49 than a standalone moment.

jokes.text: Tobias to George Sr.: 'I'm here to study with you, to learn from you. Teach me.' George Sr.: 'There's no teaching.' Tobias: 'There's no teaching.' — both immediately echoing the gang leader's decree
jokes.setup: The gang leader has just forbidden George Sr. from teaching anyone
jokes.punchline: George Sr. passes the prohibition directly to Tobias ('There's no teaching'), and Tobias dutifully repeats it back ('There's no teaching') as if accepting a studio note
jokes.explanation: The rapid pass of the prohibition is comedically efficient. Tobias's immediate acceptance of 'there's no teaching' mirrors the way actors defer to directors. The repetition works as both a callback and character comedy.

jokes.text: Tobias introduces himself: 'I am Dr. Tobias Fünke. Or, with your help, Frightened Inmate #2.' — then: 'Who is this shiny building of a man?' / 'Oh, I'm very scared right now.' / 'Oh, we're starting.'
jokes.setup: Tobias introduces himself to a large inmate in prison
jokes.punchline: The large inmate asks 'Who is this shiny building of a man?' — a bizarrely poetic compliment — and Tobias immediately interprets it as a frightening encounter and snaps into character
jokes.explanation: 'Shiny building of a man' is an exceptional, original piece of absurdist writing — a compliment so unexpected it's disorienting. Then Tobias's immediate pivot to 'Oh, I'm very scared right now' / 'Oh, we're starting' — treating real life as a rehearsal — is beautifully in character. Two great jokes compressed into one exchange. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Annyong: 'He no have father? Older lady adopt him too?' — about George Michael's absent dad
jokes.setup: Maeby has said Michael doesn't want to spend time with George Michael
jokes.punchline: Annyong interprets George Michael's situation as matching his own (orphaned, adopted by an older woman) — drawing a sharp parallel between George Michael and himself
jokes.explanation: Annyong's broken-English framing of Michael's neglect as equivalent to literal orphanhood is dark and precise. 'Older lady adopt him too?' — the parallel between Lucille (who adopted Annyong) and Michael (who is emotionally absent) is a knife hidden in a malaprop. Quietly devastating.

jokes.text: George Michael insists: 'No, he have father. Father no love him.' / Immediately corrects: 'No, he love me... loves me. He loves me.'
jokes.setup: Annyong has suggested Michael doesn't love George Michael
jokes.punchline: George Michael first accepts the framing in Annyong's broken syntax ('father no love him'), then catches himself and over-corrects with repeated 'He loves me'
jokes.explanation: The accidental adoption of Annyong's grammar ('father no love him') reveals George Michael's momentary belief that it's true. The rapid self-correction and triple repetition of 'He loves me' has the cadence of someone convincing themselves. Cringe comedy with real emotional undertow.

jokes.text: Annyong: 'I went to beach once. Next thing I know, I'm in crate next to pig.'
jokes.setup: George Michael mentions going to the beach to relax
jokes.punchline: Annyong casually explains that his last beach trip resulted in being shipped in a crate alongside a pig — strongly implying he was smuggled into the country
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The casual, matter-of-fact delivery of an extremely dark revelation (human trafficking/illegal immigration in a children's sitcom context) is peak AD. The detail of the 'pig' is perfectly chosen — specific enough to be vivid, absurd enough to keep it comedy. The deadpan economy — no setup, no explanation, just the anecdote — is extraordinary. 'Next thing I know' implies a bewildered nonchalance that makes it even funnier. This is what 9+ looks like in this episode.

jokes.text: Lucille arrives home to an empty house, calls for everyone ('Lupe! Buster? Annyong? Hello?'), then realizes she's alone and immediately calls another housekeeper: 'Hola. Is Rosa still alive? No? Hmm. Okay. This is not my day.'
jokes.setup: Lucille returns to find no one home — Lupe is at the reunion, everyone else is working
jokes.punchline: She immediately pivots to calling an alternate housekeeper named Rosa and asks if she's 'still alive' — when told no, responds 'Hmm. Okay. This is not my day.'
jokes.explanation: Layered Lucille comedy. First, she can't unload her own car — a class joke. Then she calls a backup housekeeper named Rosa and asks 'Is Rosa still alive?' with total pragmatism. The answer is no and her response — 'Hmm. Okay. This is not my day.' — treats a human death as a scheduling inconvenience. This is Lucille at her most monstrous and funniest. 'This is not my day' is enormously quotable.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Hola. Is Rosa still alive? No? Hmm. Okay. This is not my day.'
jokes.setup: Lucille, unable to find anyone to help, calls another household employee (Rosa)
jokes.punchline: Rosa has apparently died — and Lucille's reaction is 'Hmm. Okay. This is not my day.' — ranking a person's death equivalent to the inconvenience of no help with groceries
jokes.explanation: One of Lucille's finest moments in the episode. Learning a human being has died and responding 'Hmm. Okay. This is not my day.' is so perfectly sociopathic and so precisely Lucille that it almost defies improvement. The casual 'Hmm' processing a death, followed by the pivot to self-pity, is exceptional dark comedy. Maximum character integration — this line literally could not be said by any other character on any other show. Earns 8+ scores.

jokes.text: Michael 'relaxes' at the beach by working on subdivision plans in the sand, barking orders at his son: 'These units aren't too close together. We can get away with this, right, people? All right. Just speed it up. Come on. Keep your heads down. Power through.'
jokes.setup: Michael has been told to relax and spends the day at the beach
jokes.punchline: He recreates his office work in the sand, using his phone as a digging tool, commanding George Michael with the same corporate language he uses on construction workers
jokes.explanation: A perfect visual joke inferred from dialogue — Michael has built a sand model of his construction project and is treating beach time as an extension of work. The callback of 'power through, keep your heads down' on a literal beach is the joke's engine. Lindsay's diagnosis ('you'd have no reason to live') is immediately confirmed. Strong character integration — only Michael would do this.

jokes.text: Gob to construction workers: 'Screw this! I have my pride. I don't care how much they're paying me.' Worker: 'You got paid? I thought we were all making sacrifices.'
jokes.setup: Gob is trying to rally the workers to stop work
jokes.punchline: His 'I don't care how much they're paying me' inadvertently reveals he got paid — while everyone else was told to sacrifice and work without pay
jokes.explanation: Gob's self-defeating oratory — accidentally revealing he got paid while rallying unpaid workers — is perfectly constructed. His statement of principle ('I don't care how much they're paying me') undercuts his entire rebel stance. The worker's confused 'You got paid?' is the perfect button. Strong structure.

jokes.text: Gob tries to incite a work stoppage: 'I say we shut down construction!' Buster: 'This is fun! We should do this just for the fun.' Worker: 'Easy for him to say. He got his paycheck too.'
jokes.setup: Gob, who has been getting cash under the table, tries to lead a worker revolt over unpaid wages
jokes.punchline: Buster cheers enthusiastically for entirely the wrong reasons (it's 'fun'), and a worker immediately undercuts Gob's credibility by noting he got paid
jokes.explanation: Three simultaneous beats — Gob's self-interested revolt, Buster's cheerful obliviousness, and the worker's sharp undercut — work in quick succession. Buster's 'just for the fun' is the standout element, completely detached from the stakes.

jokes.text: Gob: 'He'll never stand up to the man. He's a chicken. A chicken.' — workers call Buster a chicken, Buster responds: 'My whole life you've called me a chicken, but that's over now. I have nothing to prove.'
jokes.setup: Workers are calling Buster a chicken for not joining the stoppage
jokes.punchline: Buster declares he has 'nothing to prove' — meaning he won't be goaded — but this is a character who moments later will immediately play 'chicken' because he can't resist the challenge
jokes.explanation: Buster's 'I have nothing to prove' immediately precedes him playing a dangerous game of chicken to prove exactly that. The dramatic irony is the joke. Also ties into the show's broader chicken gag (Gob's infamous chicken dance is a series-level running gag). Rewatch bonus for the immediate irony.

jokes.text: Buster: 'That is not how a chicken sounds. Chickens don't clap!' — reacting to someone doing a chicken impression
jokes.setup: Workers are calling Buster a chicken and doing chicken impressions to taunt him
jokes.punchline: Buster's objection is not to the insult but to the ornithological inaccuracy — 'chickens don't clap' — suggesting he has strong opinions about what constitutes a correct chicken impression
jokes.explanation: The callback to the show's running chicken impression gag (Gob does an infamous wrong impression) is directly invoked here — Buster's complaint that 'chickens don't clap' mirrors the same joke structure. But it also stands alone as a perfect Buster moment: his objection is entirely misplaced (the insult should bother him, not the impression's accuracy). Highly quotable and very rewatchable.

jokes.text: A construction worker announces they'll settle the dispute with a game called 'chicken' — Buster and Gob race forklifts
jokes.setup: Buster has just declared he has nothing to prove; the workers call him a chicken
jokes.punchline: A full forklift chicken race is set up with total seriousness as the industrial workplace method of conflict resolution
jokes.explanation: The escalation from verbal chicken accusations to an actual game of forklift chicken is well-constructed absurdism. The deadpan framing — 'we've got our own way of finding out who's a chicken' — treats this as standard industrial dispute resolution.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Uh-oh. My seat doesn't have a cushion.' / after the race: 'I'm definitely gonna need a cushion.'
jokes.setup: Buster is about to race a forklift in a high-stakes chicken game
jokes.punchline: His only concern — before and after the death-defying stunt — is the lack of a seat cushion, suggesting the comfort issue is more pressing than the danger
jokes.explanation: The cushion complaint bookending the most dangerous thing Buster has ever done is a perfect character joke. His threat hierarchy (discomfort > mortal danger) is consistently Buster. The repetition of the cushion line after the race (implying he won or survived) without any comment on the near-death experience is beautifully deadpan.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Where is my phone?' George Michael: 'I used it to dig the B unit.' Michael: 'You what?' 'I'll get it.' 'No, no, you're gonna ruin it!'
jokes.setup: Michael has been using his phone for business calls while at the beach; George Michael is helping with the sand model
jokes.punchline: George Michael has buried Michael's phone in the sand to 'dig the B unit' — Michael's first instinct is not to save the phone but to protect the structural integrity of the sand model
jokes.explanation: Michael's priorities are exactly wrong — saving the sand subdivision over the phone. The escalation from 'where's my phone' to 'you're gonna ruin it!' (meaning the sand model) is a perfect character moment. George Michael's earnest helpfulness that backfires is also character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Michael is on the phone panicking about the subdivision caving in — cut to his sand castle collapsing into a 'giant pool'
jokes.setup: Michael is on the phone saying 'everything's gonna fall apart, this whole thing is gonna cave in, and we're gonna be left with a bunch of...'
jokes.punchline: His sand subdivision collapses into what George Michael describes as a 'giant pool' — which happens to be his actual solution to the zoning problem (a communal pool)
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best structural jokes. Michael's panicked metaphor becomes literally true in the sand model simultaneously; and the 'cave in' accidentally creates the communal pool that solves his actual business problem. The physical visual (sand castle collapsing), the verbal irony (phone panic matching), and the plot resolution all coincide. This is textbook AD layered comedy — three things happening at once. The structure score reflects a near-perfect joke architecture.

jokes.text: Michael on the phone: 'Great. Now we're never gonna finish phase three... This whole thing is gonna cave in... Great. Now my sand castle has a giant pool in it.'
jokes.setup: Michael is on the phone having a tense work conversation about the construction project collapsing
jokes.punchline: His language about the project ('cave in,' 'giant pool') is simultaneously describing his sand castle, which has literally just collapsed to form a pool
jokes.explanation: A masterfully constructed dual-meaning moment. Michael's real estate vocabulary ('cave in,' 'giant pool in the center') maps perfectly onto the sand castle disaster happening in real time. The final line — 'Great. Now my sand castle has a giant pool in it' — works as both a literal description of the ruined sand castle AND the solution to his zoning problem (communal pool) that he announces minutes later. This is AD at its most densely constructed. Earns high scores.

jokes.text: George Michael (at the office) finds the proof that Michael loves him (presumably a note or file) — the investigators see it: 'There's the kid. Our cover's blown. Get out.'
jokes.setup: The narrator says George Michael 'found proof that his father always loved him' — the investigators planted surveillance equipment earlier
jokes.punchline: The 'proof of love' George Michael finds is an FBI evidence file — his father's love is documented evidence of corporate fraud
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed ironic gag. The narrator's warm framing ('he was holding the proof in his hands') collides with the revelation that this 'proof of love' is actually federal evidence against his father. The show reframes paternal devotion as criminal liability. This is the kind of dark structural irony AD does better than any other show.

jokes.text: After the forklift chicken race: 'Wow. Who won?' — and 'for the first time, Gob saw his brother as a man'
jokes.setup: The forklift chicken race has apparently concluded off-screen
jokes.punchline: The result is so ambiguous that a bystander has to ask 'Who won?' — and the narrator sentimentally reports Gob's newfound respect for Buster without explaining what happened
jokes.explanation: The deadpan 'who won?' after a dramatic buildup, followed by the sentimental narrator beat delivered straight, is a structurally AD joke. The lack of resolution (we never find out who won or what happened) is the joke. The narrator's sincere emotional note played over an unexplained industrial accident is very in-key for the show.

jokes.text: After the forklift chicken showdown, narrative beat: 'For the first time, Gob saw his brother as a man.'
jokes.setup: Buster has 'won' the forklift chicken game through accidental discomfort
jokes.punchline: The narrator frames Buster's accidental victory as a genuine coming-of-age moment — Gob seeing Buster 'as a man' for the first time based on completely hollow grounds
jokes.explanation: The narrator's sentimental framing of an absurd event as a genuine growth moment is a recurring AD technique and it works well here. The gap between 'accidental cushion victory' and 'saw his brother as a man' is the joke.

jokes.text: Lindsay arrives at the construction site and threatens workers with 'a stair car full of Mexican laborers.' A 'laborer' responds: 'I'm a professor of American Studies at the University of Mexico City.' Another worker: 'I took a dance class there.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay threatens to replace striking workers with Lupe's family from the stair car
jokes.punchline: Her 'scab laborers' turn out to be academics — 'a professor of American Studies' — who are deeply insulted; a construction worker's non-sequitur 'I took a dance class there' is perfect
jokes.explanation: A multilayered payoff. Lindsay's classism backfires spectacularly — her 'laborers' are educated professionals. The professor's outraged dignity is great. But the construction worker's perfectly placed 'I took a dance class there' is the topper — an irrelevant personal connection that completely derails the tension. The callback to Lupe's family (set up 15 minutes earlier) makes this a true AD structural joke. One of the episode's best.

jokes.text: Lupe's family: 'I thought you were taking us to Catalina. What happened to Catalina?'
jokes.setup: Lupe's family has ended up at the construction site after being rerouted by Lindsay
jokes.punchline: They reveal they got on Lindsay's bus thinking it was headed to Catalina — a callback to the original mix-up when the employees boarded their bus — now the confusion has rippled back to Lupe's family
jokes.explanation: A clean callback closing the loop on the bus mix-up. The joke is that both groups (employees and Lupe's family) have been confused about where they're going, creating a perfect comic symmetry. Not exceptionally crafted but structurally satisfying.

jokes.text: Gob: 'You'd bring in scabs, just like that?' Lindsay: 'Watch me.' — then Michael arrives and overrides everything
jokes.setup: Lindsay is playing hard-line manager; Gob is the supposed workers' champion
jokes.punchline: The role reversal — Lindsay threatening scabs while Gob defends the workers — is the joke, given their characters
jokes.explanation: The role reversal (Lindsay as management hardliner, Gob as worker advocate) is character comedy, but it's in service of setup rather than its own punchline. Functional.

jokes.text: Michael's solution to the zoning problem (no individual pools, one big communal pool) is revealed to have been inspired by his sand castle collapsing
jokes.setup: The sand castle collapse earlier accidentally created what looked like a pool
jokes.punchline: Michael presents this as a genuine business solution, having been inadvertently inspired by George Michael burying his phone in the sand
jokes.explanation: Closes the loop on the beach subplot beautifully. The business solution accidentally discovered through play is the kind of thematic and structural payoff AD excels at. The irony that Michael needed to 'stop working' to solve his work problem, and he did it while working anyway, is the actual joke.

jokes.text: Michael tells Lindsay she was right, suggests rewarding staff with a party; Lindsay: 'Right. I didn't mean tonight. Just some time in the future when we have some extra food and liquor.' — then immediately yells 'Lupe! I need help with the groceries!'
jokes.setup: Michael acknowledges Lindsay's management instincts and suggests rewarding hard work
jokes.punchline: Lindsay immediately proves she has no intention of following through on her own advice — and then calls for Lupe (who she just sent to Catalina) to carry her groceries
jokes.explanation: The callback to Lupe being given the day off makes Lindsay's automatic 'Lupe!' all the better — she's calling for someone who, thanks to Michael earlier, is now on a bus to Catalina. Also: 'some extra food and liquor' perfectly reveals her management philosophy is performative. Solid character work.

jokes.text: Gob: 'How about just liquor?'
jokes.setup: Michael has suggested rewarding the workers with food and liquor at a party 'in the future'
jokes.punchline: Gob negotiates the food part away — 'just liquor'
jokes.explanation: Two words, perfectly Gob. The negotiation of food out of the party package is character-consistent and economical. Not a sophisticated joke but very well placed and delivered.

jokes.text: Michael and George Michael finally relax together; Michael: 'Thanks for coming out. I've been dying to get some time with you.' George Michael: 'That's okay. I know how much you care about me.' Michael: 'Unfortunately, so does the federal government.'
jokes.setup: Father and son have a genuine bonding moment; George Michael says he knows Michael loves him
jokes.punchline: Michael agrees — and then reveals that the federal government also knows, because it's documented in an evidence file (which George Michael was just holding)
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke and a genuine AD classic. The warm father-son moment is turned on its head in four words — 'Unfortunately, so does the federal government' — which simultaneously closes the evidence file subplot, recontextualizes George Michael finding 'proof of love,' and delivers the episode's darkest laugh. The structure is perfect: a beat of warmth followed by the most unexpected possible intrusion of legal jeopardy into the emotional payoff. High quotability, strong rewatch bonus. This is what AD does that no other show does.

jokes.text: "On the next Arrested Development" tag: Tobias in prison learns about bunking. George Sr.: 'You'll be sleeping under me for a while. I sold you for a pack of cigarettes.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is bunking with George Sr. in prison
jokes.punchline: George Sr. has sold Tobias to another prisoner for cigarettes — a classic prison economy joke applied to a family member, with the additional layer that it's Tobias (oblivious) being sold
jokes.explanation: The 'next time' tag delivers an exceptional joke. 'I sold you for a pack of cigarettes' is the perfect dark punchline — it uses the prison economy cliché but applies it to George Sr. selling his own son-in-law, which is both consistent with his character and shocking. The setup 'You'll be sleeping under me for a while' also carries Tobias's inevitable double entendre weight even without Tobias saying it.

jokes.text: NEXT EPISODE preview: Tobias: 'Well, let's discuss this bunking situation. You'll be sleeping under me for a while.' George Sr.: 'I sold you for a pack of cigarettes.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has been placed in George Sr.'s cell as a method-acting researcher
jokes.punchline: George Sr. has already sold Tobias to another inmate for cigarettes — the ultimate expression of how little he values his son-in-law, and also a dark prison-economy joke
jokes.explanation: Though from the preview, this is an extremely well-crafted joke. 'I sold you for a pack of cigarettes' does maximum work — it deploys prison economy, reveals George Sr.'s complete indifference to Tobias, and uses the bunk setup (Tobias sleeping 'under' George Sr.) as dark foreshadowing. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: NEXT EPISODE preview: The employees are lost on a mountain. A cowboy offers them a ride back to town on horses: 'Come on. Let's go. Buck, Banjo, get on up here.'
jokes.setup: The employees got on the wrong bus to Catalina and are now apparently lost on a mountain
jokes.punchline: The resolution involves a cowboy and horses — the employees have traveled from office workers to mountain-lost to horse-riding in the span of one episode's chaos
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'wrong bus' to 'lost on a mountain' to 'rescued by a cowboy' is absurdist escalation at its best. The preview itself is a joke — the deadpan revelation of where the employees ended up. Buck and Banjo as horse names are perfect comic details.

tmdb_overview: When Michael discovers his family has been receiving paychecks without working, he forces them all to get jobs within the company. Lindsay questions his management skills, and Tobias checks himself in at George Sr.'s prison to research a film role.
insight: Meticulous character work drives 49 jokes in 24 minutes—escalation comedy at its tightest.
